commands.repair_reason: structured ClawSweeper needs-human verdict with repairable P-severity findings (sha=e59c7a7e773a6974ce4feda32cc487c402d8bc55)
commands.review_summary:
Adds a stacked channel-broker SDK/plugin/docs/test surface plus Telegram topic target normalization and canonical broker session-route proof for opt-in broker routing.

PR surface: Source +2001, Tests +1370, Docs +252, Config +78, Generated 0, Other +29. Total +3730 across 50 files.

Reproducibility: yes. for the PR defects: source inspection shows the missing runtime export path and the schema/type mismatch. This is not a current-main user bug reproduction because the channel-broker feature is new in the PR.
commands.review_followup: This needs contributor-supplied real behavior proof plus maintainer stack/API direction; the code defects are clear, but automation cannot satisfy the proof gate for the contributor's environment.

target.title: [Feature]: Channel Broker Phase 2B Telegram migration proof
target.body:
## Maintainer TL;DR This stack consolidates the recurring Telegram/Discord/Slack/WhatsApp/Signal/iMessage/etc. maintenance churn behind one Channel Broker contract. OpenClaw keeps the shared semantics that repeatedly regress across native channel plugins: sessions, allowlists, routing, /verbose, streaming policy, durable final sends, receipts, retries, and auditability. Broker providers own platform mechanics: bot APIs, bridge/device state, native ids, delivery quirks, and provider capabilities. This PR proves Telegram topics can move through broker-owned target parsing without changing native Telegram defaults. It is intentionally an opt-in proof layer, not the default migration. The direction is intentionally incremental: first land the versioned broker SDK/plugin foundation, then prove conformance, then prove opt-in routing for the major channels, then model long-tail and constrained providers. Only after migration paths exist should legacy native-channel feature work freeze in #86115. ## Why This Is Better Today, one fix to streaming, /verbose, or delivery behavior can make Telegram fast this week and then break Discord, Slack, or another native plugin the next week. The broker path makes those shared semantics one OpenClaw-owned contract instead of many platform-specific re-implementations. When a provider adds support for previews, threads, receipts, media, or constrained account modes, that capability lands through the same contract and becomes available to every routed channel without OpenClaw maintainers owning every platform quirk. ## Stack Shape ```mermaid flowchart TD U["#86105 Umbrella: reduce maintained-channel churn"] --> P1["#86096 Phase 1: SDK + bundled broker foundation"] P1 --> P2A["#86153 Phase 2A: conformance + signed inbound"] P2A --> P2B["#86154 Phase 2B: Telegram opt-in proof"] P2B --> P2C["#86156 Phase 2C: Discord opt-in proof"] P2C --> P2D["#86157 Phase 2D: Slack opt-in proof"] P2D --> P3["#86164 Phase 3: official/app capability matrix"] P3 --> P4["#86165 Phase 4: constrained provider capabilities"] P4 -. "next, not in this stack" .-> P5["#86115 Phase 5: legacy native-plugin freeze"] ``` | PR | Role in the consolidation | | --- | --- | | #86096 | Creates the versioned broker SDK/plugin foundation, provider routing guard, media final-send mapping, SecretRef/cancellation handling, and prefix-ownership contract. | | #86153 | Proves shared lifecycle conformance and adds signed inbound webhook registration/runtime handoff: inbound dedupe, durable final sends, receipts, retryable failures, preview/progress gates, and /verbose channelData. | | #86154 | Proves Telegram topics can route through broker targets without native Telegram internals. | | #86156 | Proves Discord DMs/channels/threads preserve conversation semantics through broker routing. | | #86157 | Proves Slack DMs/channel threads preserve direct/thread semantics through broker routing. | | #86164 | Adds official/app platform aliases, capability matrix rows, and declared delivery capability enforcement for long-tail channels. | | #86165 | Adds constrained-provider metadata for WhatsApp, Signal, iMessage, and bridge-backed providers. | ## Ownership Model ```mermaid flowchart LR Core["OpenClaw core
sessions, routing, allowlists
/verbose, streaming, retries
receipts, audit"] --> Broker["channel-broker plugin
openclaw/plugin-sdk/channel-broker
V1 protocol"] Broker --> Provider["Provider-owned broker
HTTP/webhook transport
platform adapters"] Provider --> Platforms["Telegram, Discord, Slack
Teams, Matrix, WhatsApp
Signal, iMessage, long tail"] Provider --> Broker Broker --> Core ``` ## Review Shape These PRs are intentionally linear. GitHub still shows `main` as the base for each PR because the current credentials cannot create the upstream base refs needed for true stacked PR bases; the fork branches are stacked and maintainers can review the focused adjacent diffs here: | PR | Focused adjacent diff | | --- | --- | | #86153 | https://github.com/100yenadmin/openclaw/compare/feat/channel-broker-protocol...feat/channel-broker-conformance | | #86154 | https://github.com/100yenadmin/openclaw/compare/feat/channel-broker-conformance...feat/channel-broker-telegram-routing | | #86156 | https://github.com/100yenadmin/openclaw/compare/feat/channel-broker-telegram-routing...feat/channel-broker-discord-routing | | #86157 | https://github.com/100yenadmin/openclaw/compare/feat/channel-broker-discord-routing...feat/channel-broker-slack-routing | | #86164 | https://github.com/100yenadmin/openclaw/compare/feat/channel-broker-slack-routing...feat/channel-broker-official-platform-capabilities | | #86165 | https://github.com/100yenadmin/openclaw/compare/feat/channel-broker-official-platform-capabilities...feat/channel-broker-constrained-platform-capabilities | ## This PR - Phase: Phase 2B - Telegram opt-in broker routing proof - Role: Proves Telegram topics can route through broker targets without native Telegram internals. - Head: `e59c7a7e77` ## Current Review State This PR is live for maintainer review. Current head: `e59c7a7e77`. Focused local validation for this phase is documented below. Broad validation remains owned by GitHub Actions. Native channel defaults remain unchanged unless this phase explicitly says otherwise. ## Summary - Adds the Phase 2B Telegram broker routing proof on top of #86153 and #86096. - Normalizes Telegram topic shorthand in the broker plugin, including `broker:telegram::topic:` and numeric `:` targets. - Canonicalizes outbound broker session routes to `telegram:?threadId=` so topic routing is stable without importing native Telegram internals. ## Rollout Context Closes #86110 Related #86108 Related #86105 Stacked on #86153 Stacked on #86096 Supersedes closed PR #86137, which was closed by the repository active-PR cap rather than by an implementation failure. Native Telegram defaults stay unchanged. Existing target-prefix ownership still keeps `telegram:` owned by the native Telegram plugin when that plugin is installed; this PR proves the opt-in broker route through `broker:telegram:...` and the broker alias path when native Telegram is absent. For review, the focused stack diff is: https://github.com/100yenadmin/openclaw/compare/feat/channel-broker-conformance...feat/channel-broker-telegram-routing ## Real Behavior Proof - **Behavior or issue addressed:** Telegram topic targets can route through `channel-broker` and become provider requests with canonical `telegram` platform, channel conversation id, and topic `threadId`. - **Real environment tested:** Local OpenClaw checkout at `/Volumes/LEXAR/repos/openclaw-channel-broker`, using the Channel Broker plugin and SDK from the stacked rollout branch on May 24, 2026. - **Exact steps or command run after this patch:** Ran a `node --import tsx --input-type=module` terminal proof that called `channelBrokerPlugin.message.send.text` with `to: "broker:telegram:-1001234567890:topic:42"` and captured the provider request passed to the configured broker runtime. - **Evidence after fix:** Terminal output excerpt from that local command: ```json { "label": "telegram-topic", "messageId": "native-telegram-topic", "platform": "telegram", "conversation": { "id": "-1001234567890", "type": "channel", "threadId": "42" }, "requirements": { "text": true, "thread": true } } ``` - **Observed result after fix:** The live plugin path normalized Telegram topic shorthand into the broker provider request shape without importing native Telegram extension internals. - **What was not tested:** Live Telegram Bot API traffic and the full local suite; this PR stays scoped to opt-in broker routing proof. ### Behavior OpenClaw can route a Telegram topic through `channel-broker` without Telegram extension code. The broker receives a normalized platform `telegram`, conversation id, and thread id; OpenClaw keeps the session route canonical and leaves native Telegram prefix ownership intact. ### Environment - Local checkout: `/Volumes/LEXAR/repos/openclaw-channel-broker` - Branch: `feat/channel-broker-telegram-routing` - Head: `5553962ba6 Add Telegram broker routing proof` ### Steps 1. Sent a synthetic message to `broker:telegram:-1001234567890:topic:42` through `channelBrokerPlugin.message.send.text`. 2. Verified the provider request used `platform: "telegram"`, `conversation.id: "-1001234567890"`, and `conversation.threadId: "42"`. 3. Resolved the outbound session route for the same target. 4. Verified the route canonicalized to `telegram:-1001234567890?threadId=42` and preserved channel chat semantics. ### Evidence - `pnpm exec vitest run extensions/channel-broker/src/channel.test.ts extensions/channel-broker/src/conformance.test.ts --config test/vitest/vitest.extension-channel-broker.config.ts` - 2 files passed, 10 tests passed. - `pnpm exec tsc --noEmit -p extensions/channel-broker/tsconfig.json` - passed. - `pnpm exec oxfmt --check extensions/channel-broker/src/target.ts extensions/channel-broker/src/channel.ts extensions/channel-broker/src/channel.test.ts` - passed. - `pnpm exec oxlint extensions/channel-broker --format unix` - passed. - `git diff --check` - passed. ### Observed Result Telegram topic routing is represented by broker-owned target normalization and provider request semantics, not by core importing or calling native Telegram extension internals. ### Not Tested - Live Telegram Bot API traffic; this PR is the opt-in broker migration proof, not a live provider connector. - Full local suite. Per `/Volumes/LEXAR` policy, broad validation is left to GitHub Actions. ## Current Stack Gate Context This branch is rebased on the current Phase 1 foundation head. Focused local validation is documented above or below; broad validation remains on GitHub Actions.
target.author: 100yenadmin
